title: THE REFORMATION: The Greatest Revival in the Post-Apostolic Age – Bernard of Clairvaux
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
series: The Greatest Revival in the Post-Apostolic Age
source_files: THE REFORMATION_ The Greatest Revival in the Post Apostolic Age Bernard of Clairvaux - Nick Anderson.txt
outline: Introduction: Bernard’s famous quote on Christ as true man and true God
Why study Bernard? His nicknames: Honey-Tongued Doctor, Theologian of Love
The greatest preacher of his age – comparable to Edwards and Whitefield
Bernard’s monastic revival: Clairvaux and 300 daughter monasteries
Luther and Calvin’s high estimation of Bernard
Bernard’s theology of sovereign grace – ‘Calvinist before Calvin’
Bernard’s dispute with Abelard over the atonement
Bernard’s involvement in the Second Crusade and its aftermath
Bernard’s lasting influence on hymnody and Reformed theology
summary: An October Reformation series lesson on Bernard of Clairvaux (1090–1153), the most famous man in the world at his death, delivered by Nick Anderson. Called the Honey-Tongued Doctor and Theologian of Love, Bernard almost single-handedly revitalized medieval monasticism, founding Clairvaux and planting 300 monasteries across Europe. Luther called him ‘superior to all doctors in his sermons’ and Calvin quoted him 33 times in the Institutes, calling him a major witness to gospel truth in the medieval church. Known as ‘a Calvinist before Calvin,’ Bernard championed sovereign grace against Abelard’s rationalist theology of the atonement. The lesson also examines his controversial involvement in the Second Crusade.
transcript:
## Why Bernard of Clairvaux?

Bernard of Clairvaux was called "the man for all seasons," "the Honey-Tongued Doctor of the twelfth century," and "the Theologian of Love." By every account, he was the greatest preacher of his age. At the time of his death, he was the most famous man in the world. It would not be wrong to compare him in some ways to a Jonathan Edwards or a George Whitefield.

He once complained that too many people came to see him every day seeking counsel — so many that he said he did not even have time to pray. He almost single-handedly revitalized the monastic life: the monastery of Clairvaux, which he co-founded with twelve other monks, would eventually plant 300 more monasteries all over Europe, as far north as Sweden and as far east as Palestine.

We still sing his hymns: "O Sacred Head Now Wounded," "Jesus, the Very Thought of Thee," and "Jesus, Thou Joy of Loving Hearts." Both Calvin and Luther loved him deeply — which is significant, because neither had much patience with medieval Christianity in general. Luther said: "Bernard is superior to all the doctors in his sermons, even to Augustine himself, because he preaches Christ most excellently." And: "Bernard loved Jesus as much as any man can. Bernard was the greatest Christian in the Middle Ages." Calvin quotes Bernard 33 times in the *Institutes* and writes that "Bernard speaks as though the very truth itself were speaking."

## Historical Background: The Eleventh Century

The eleventh century was a significant turning point in the church. In 1054, the official split between the Eastern and Western churches occurred, driven by two controversies: Pope Leo IX asserting authority over the entire world through the forged "Donation of Constantine," and the disagreement over whether the Holy Spirit proceeds from the Father and the Son. Unable to resolve these issues, East and West excommunicated each other.

During this same period, the Pope was becoming increasingly influential in politics. A famous showdown between Pope Gregory VII and Emperor Henry IV ended with Henry standing barefoot in the snow for three days at the Pope's door, begging for restoration to the church's communion. The Pope's ecclesiastical authority was dramatically demonstrated.

## The Life of Bernard

Bernard of Clairvaux was not from Clairvaux — he was born in 1090 at the Chateau de Fontaines (the chateau still stands today as a memorial to him). He died in 1153 at the age of sixty-three.

He was born into nobility. His father wanted all six sons to become knights. His mother, however, had a different wish for Bernard: that he would be devoted to the Lord. When his mother died while he was seventeen, Bernard resolved to fulfill her dying wish.

In 1112, he entered a newly formed and very strict monastery. Three years later — remarkably quickly — he was appointed Abbot of a new daughter house. He took twelve monks to a remote, swampy valley and founded the monastery of Clairvaux. That monastery still exists, though it is now a prison, converted during the French Revolution.

His father was initially deeply disappointed that his son would not become a knight. But in the course of time, all of Bernard's brothers and his father eventually joined him at the monastery as monks.

Bernard was extremely strict — strict with others, but even stricter with himself. The result was permanent damage to his health; he suffered from severe digestive problems for the rest of his life and required special accommodations during church services. Yet despite his frail health, he accomplished more than most men in history before or after him. (Calvin similarly regretted at the end of his life that he had pushed his body too hard.) Bernard himself established between sixty and seventy additional monasteries, and that work was continued after his death.

## Bernard's Theology: A Calvinist Before Calvinism

Bernard was a Calvinist before Calvinism existed. Some examples:

On Christ's intercession and eternal security: "He bore the sins of many, and prayed for the transgressors — so that they may not perish. But they cannot perish for whom the Son prays and for whom the Father delivers them to His own Son to procure their life."

On the bondage of the will: "Man's fall... is not to be ascribed to the gift of the power to sin, but to the fall of the will. Nevertheless, though he fell by an act of will, he does not have it equally in his power by an act of will to rise again free from sin. For it is not so easy to get out of the pit as it is to fall into it. By an act of will alone, man fell into the pit of sin; but no act of will is sufficient to enable him to rise again, seeing that now even if he so will, he is not able to not sin."

These are Augustinian convictions, and they go back through Augustine to the Apostle Paul.

On the Scriptures: "The scriptures need to be read in the same spirit as the way in which they were written. You will never enter into Paul's meaning until by reading him with constant application and by giving yourself to constant meditation you invite his spirit. Likewise you will never understand David until by your own personal experience you have made the very sentiments of the Psalms your own."

## Bernard's Public Ministry

Bernard was a monk who ideally wanted to be a hermit — yet he found himself repeatedly drawn into the affairs of the world. When a papal dispute arose between two rival claimants, Bernard publicly supported Innocent II, and in no small part because of his speeches, Innocent II prevailed and became the next Pope. This further spread Bernard's fame across Europe.

He also entered into controversy with the philosopher Pierre Abelard, one of the most influential intellectuals of the twelfth century. Abelard had turned the Augustinian principle of "faith seeking understanding" on its head, demanding understanding before faith. This affected his theology seriously: Abelard taught that Christ did not die to pay a ransom but merely to demonstrate how far love should go.

Bernard responded: "I was made a sinner by deriving my being from Adam; I am made just by being washed in the blood of Christ. Shall generation from a sinner be sufficient to condemn me, and shall not the blood of Christ be sufficient to justify me?" At the Council of Sens in 1140, Bernard's opening statement was so powerful that Abelard himself did not respond — he simply withdrew. His teachings were condemned and confirmed by the Pope.

## The Second Crusade

The probable low point in Bernard's life was his involvement in the Second Crusade (1145-1149). His former pupil became Pope Eugenius III, and at the Pope's request, Bernard traveled throughout Europe preaching and promoting the crusade. He raised approximately 70,000 men. The crusade was a dismal failure — fewer than 7,000 men survived, and nothing was accomplished.

Bernard's popularity suffered. He tried to some degree to distance himself from the failure, saying that like Moses in the wilderness, it was the sins of the people — not the leader — that brought judgment. But it was not his finest hour.

## The Man and His Legacy

If Bernard had been born four hundred years later, he would almost certainly have been a reformer. He was distraught by what he saw going on in the church of his day. He denied the immaculate conception of Mary — saying he could find no such doctrine in Scripture. He denied that baptism was necessary for salvation, insisting that faith in Christ alone was sufficient — a significant stand in his time. He opposed papal tyranny, writing to his former pupil Pope Eugenius III: "You are despicable in all circumstances; you know no law but your pleasure; you never think of God nor do you fear him... A ministry has been imposed upon us rather than a dominion bestowed."

Bernard's goal in preaching was not so much to explain words as to reach people's hearts. As Calvin and Luther recognized, he was right on the most critical theological issues: the nature of the atonement, the bondage of the will, the necessity of grace, faith alone. His shortcomings were real — his allegorical interpretation of Scripture, his mysticism, his role in the Crusade. But one can trace a line from Bernard to Wycliffe, then to Hus, and on into the Reformation. The Lord works through imperfect men, and that gives us confidence that he can work through us as well.
